name: Male
summary: Male, the capital of the Maldives, is relatively safe for tourists with low violent crime rates, though visitors should remain alert for petty theft and occasional gang-related incidents. The city follows strict Islamic customs requiring modest dress and behavior, particularly outside resort areas.
quickVerdict: Male is generally safe for tourists with proper precautions. While violent crime is rare and mainly gang-related affecting locals rather than visitors, petty theft does occur in crowded areas. The US State Department maintains a Level 2 advisory due to terrorism concerns, though incidents remain isolated. Visitors should dress modestly, respect Islamic customs, and exercise standard urban safety practices. Resort islands offer higher security levels than the capital.

neighborhoods.name: Henveiru
neighborhoods.description: The main commercial district with shops, cafes, and the fish market. Generally safe during daylight hours with good foot traffic. Stay alert for pickpockets in crowded market areas.

neighborhoods.name: Machangolhi
neighborhoods.description: Central residential area with government buildings and offices. Well-patrolled and relatively safe throughout the day. Good for exploring local culture and dining.

neighborhoods.name: Galolhu
neighborhoods.description: Northern district with local housing and smaller shops. Generally safe but can be quieter at night. Stick to main streets after dark and dress modestly.

neighborhoods.name: Machchangolhi South
neighborhoods.description: Mixed residential and commercial area. Some reports of petty crime in less busy streets. Exercise caution at night and avoid displaying valuables.

neighborhoods.name: Hulhumale
neighborhoods.description: Modern planned city connected by bridge. Well-developed with good lighting and security. Popular with tourists and generally very safe day and night.

neighborhoods.name: Villingili
neighborhoods.description: Separate island accessible by ferry. Quieter residential area with limited tourist facilities. Exercise caution when exploring alone, particularly in the evening.

scams.name: Restaurant Commission Scam
scams.description: Well-dressed individuals on motorbikes approach tourists offering to show good restaurants, then lead them to establishments where they receive commission and tourists pay inflated prices from a special tourist menu.
scams.howToAvoid: Politely decline unsolicited restaurant recommendations from strangers. Research restaurants in advance and ask locals for genuine recommendations. Check if restaurants have different menus or prices for tourists versus locals.

scams.name: Fake Tour Operator Bookings
scams.description: Fraudulent travel companies create fake websites to collect payments for liveaboards, resorts, or excursions that don't exist, leaving tourists stranded at the airport without valid bookings.
scams.howToAvoid: Book only through verified operators or official resort websites. Avoid wire transfers or large upfront payments. Verify company credentials and read recent reviews from multiple sources before booking.

scams.name: IMUGA Declaration Form Scam
scams.description: Fake websites claim to offer the mandatory IMUGA traveler declaration form for a fee, when the official form is completely free and only available through the government website.
scams.howToAvoid: Only use the official government IMUGA website for traveler declarations. Be suspicious of any site requesting payment for government forms. Verify URLs carefully and look for official government domains.

scams.name: ATM Skimming
scams.description: Card skimming devices installed on ATMs, particularly at less monitored locations, can steal credit card information for unauthorized transactions.
scams.howToAvoid: Use ATMs at banks or trusted hotels only. Cover your PIN when entering it. Check for any unusual devices attached to the ATM. Monitor your bank statements regularly for unauthorized transactions.

soloFemale.overview: Male is relatively safe for solo female travelers who dress modestly and respect local Islamic customs. The Maldives has low violent crime rates and women are generally respected. However, as the capital follows strict Islamic laws, conservative dress covering shoulders and knees is essential outside resort areas. Solo female travelers report feeling safe but recommend avoiding isolated areas at night and staying in well-reviewed accommodations.
tips: Dress modestly with shoulders and knees covered at all times in Male - bring lightweight long sleeves and pants
Stay in reputable hotels or guesthouses with good security and reviews from other solo female travelers
Avoid walking alone at night in quiet areas - stick to well-lit main streets and busy areas
Be respectful of prayer times when shops may close briefly and avoid public displays that might be culturally insensitive
Consider staying in nearby Hulhumale which is more modern and tourist-friendly than traditional Male neighborhoods

nightSafety.overview: Male is generally safe at night but requires extra caution due to limited lighting in some areas and cultural considerations. The city becomes quieter after dark as most businesses close early and locals observe evening prayer times. While violent crime is rare, petty theft and gang activity can occur in less populated areas. Tourist areas and main streets are safer with some foot traffic until late evening.
tips: Stick to well-lit main streets and avoid wandering into residential side streets after 10 PM
Many shops and restaurants close early due to prayer times - plan evening activities accordingly
Use hotel-arranged transport or reputable taxi services rather than walking long distances at night
Avoid displaying expensive items like jewelry, cameras, or large amounts of cash when out after dark
Stay aware that as a Muslim country, nightlife is limited and alcohol is not available outside resorts

transport.metro: Male has no metro system due to its small size. The city is walkable and most attractions are within a short distance.
transport.rideshare: International rideshare apps like Uber are not available. Local taxi services operate but are limited in number due to the small size of the island.
transport.taxis: Taxis are available but limited in number. They generally don't use meters, so agree on fares beforehand. Most rides within Male cost around 25-50 MVR. Hotel staff can help arrange reliable taxi services.
transport.tips: Walking is the most common way to get around Male due to its compact size. For inter-island travel, use government ferry services, speedboats, or seaplanes arranged through resorts. Public buses operate from Male to Hulhumale and the airport bridge.

customs: Dress modestly with shoulders and knees covered in all public areas - this is strictly enforced and culturally important
Remove shoes before entering homes, mosques, and some shops - look for shoes at the entrance as a sign
Use your right hand for eating, greeting, and giving/receiving items - the left hand is considered unclean in Islamic culture
Respect prayer times when shops may close briefly - there are five daily prayers that structure local life
Avoid public displays of affection including holding hands or kissing - this is considered disrespectful in Islamic culture

health.overview: Healthcare in Male includes several hospitals but facilities are limited compared to developed countries. For serious medical emergencies, evacuation to India, Sri Lanka, or Singapore may be necessary.
health.water: Tap water is generally safe to drink in Male as it comes from desalinated sources, but bottled water is widely available and recommended for sensitive stomachs.
health.vaccinations: No mandatory vaccinations required except Yellow Fever if arriving from endemic areas. Recommended vaccinations include Hepatitis A, Hepatitis B, and Typhoid. Consult a travel health professional 4-6 weeks before departure.
health.altitude: Male is at sea level with a tropical climate. The main health concerns are sun exposure, dehydration, and waterborne illnesses. Heat and humidity can be intense - stay hydrated and use strong sunscreen.

emergency.usEmbassy: The US has no embassy in Male. US citizens should contact the US Embassy in Colombo, Sri Lanka at +94 11 249 8500 for assistance or the Department of State 24-hour line at +1-202-501-4444 from abroad.

faq.a: Yes, Male is generally safe for tourists with proper precautions. Violent crime is rare and mainly affects locals rather than visitors. However, petty theft can occur in crowded areas, and visitors should dress modestly and respect Islamic customs. The US maintains a Level 2 travel advisory primarily due to terrorism concerns, though incidents remain isolated.

Male is relatively safe at night but requires extra caution. The city becomes quieter after dark and lighting can be limited in some areas. Stick to well-lit main streets, avoid isolated areas, and use hotel-arranged transport. Most businesses close early due to prayer times, so plan evening activities accordingly.

faq.q: Is Male safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Male is generally safe for solo female travelers who respect local customs. Dress modestly with shoulders and knees covered at all times, stay in reputable accommodations, and avoid walking alone at night in quiet areas. Women are generally respected in Maldivian culture, but Islamic customs must be observed.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Male?
faq.a: Avoid quiet residential side streets at night, particularly in outer areas of the city. Some parts of Male and nearby Hulhumale have reported gang activity, though this rarely affects tourists. Stay in central commercial areas and stick to main streets. Always be cautious around less populated areas after dark.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Male?
faq.a: Public transport in Male is limited but generally safe. Buses operate to Hulhumale and the airport bridge. Taxis are few but reliable - agree on fares beforehand as meters aren't always used. Walking is the most common way to get around due to Male's small size. For inter-island travel, use official ferry services or resort-arranged transfers.
